name: Hydrogen
Key uses / notes: Fuel cells; Haber process ammonia synthesis; most abundant element in universe

name: Helium
Key uses / notes: Cryogenics; MRI superconducting magnets; lighter-than-air balloon gas

name: Lithium
Key uses / notes: Li-ion batteries; psychiatric medication (lithium carbonate); lightest metal

name: Beryllium
Key uses / notes: Aerospace alloys; X-ray windows; nuclear reactor reflectors; toxic

name: Boron
Key uses / notes: Borosilicate glass (Pyrex); semiconductors; neutron absorber in reactors

name: Carbon
Key uses / notes: Basis of organic chemistry; diamond (hardest natural material); graphite

name: Nitrogen
Key uses / notes: Fertilizers (Haber process); liquid N₂ cryogen; 78% of atmosphere

name: Oxygen
Key uses / notes: Respiration; steel making; water formation; most abundant element in Earth's crust

name: Fluorine
Key uses / notes: Toothpaste (fluoride); Teflon (PTFE); most electronegative element

name: Neon
Key uses / notes: Neon signs (orange-red glow); lasers; cryogenic research

name: Sodium
Key uses / notes: NaCl table salt; street lamp sodium vapor; biological electrolyte

name: Magnesium
Key uses / notes: Lightweight structural alloys; antacids; essential mineral in biology

name: Aluminum
Key uses / notes: Aerospace and packaging; electrical cables; most abundant metal in Earth's crust

name: Silicon
Key uses / notes: Semiconductor chips; solar cells; second most abundant element in Earth's crust

name: Phosphorus
Key uses / notes: Fertilizers (phosphoric acid); matches; DNA/RNA backbone

name: Sulfur
Key uses / notes: Sulfuric acid (most produced industrial chemical); rubber vulcanization

name: Chlorine
Key uses / notes: Water disinfection; PVC plastics; HCl production

name: Argon
Key uses / notes: Inert shielding gas for welding; incandescent bulbs; atmospheric tracer

name: Potassium
Key uses / notes: Biological electrolyte (nerve signals); fertilizers; potassium permanganate

name: Calcium
Key uses / notes: Bones and teeth (calcium phosphate); cement; biological signaling ion

name: Scandium
Key uses / notes: High-strength Al alloys; solid oxide fuel cells; rare in nature

name: Titanium
Key uses / notes: Aerospace alloys (Ti-6Al-4V); biomedical implants; corrosion resistant

name: Vanadium
Key uses / notes: High-strength steel alloys; vanadium redox flow batteries

name: Chromium
Key uses / notes: Stainless steel; chrome plating; refractory bricks; Aufbau anomaly (3d⁵ 4s¹)

name: Manganese
Key uses / notes: Steel production (desulfurizer); dry cell batteries (MnO₂); permanganate oxidizer

name: Iron
Key uses / notes: Steel and cast iron; hemoglobin (biological O₂ transport); most common metal by mass

name: Cobalt
Key uses / notes: Superalloys for jet engines; vitamin B12; permanent magnets (SmCo, AlNiCo)

name: Nickel
Key uses / notes: Stainless and alloy steels; electroplating; coins; Ni-Cd batteries

name: Copper
Key uses / notes: Electrical wiring; plumbing; coins; Aufbau anomaly (3d¹⁰ 4s¹)

name: Zinc
Key uses / notes: Galvanizing steel; brass alloys; zinc oxide sunscreen; biological enzyme cofactor

name: Gallium
Key uses / notes: Semiconductors (GaAs, GaN); low-melting alloys; melts in hand (mp 29.8°C)

name: Germanium
Key uses / notes: Fiber optics; infrared optics; predicted by Mendeleev (eka-silicon) before discovery

name: Arsenic
Key uses / notes: Wood preservatives (CCA); semiconductors; highly toxic; ancient poison

name: Selenium
Key uses / notes: Glassmaking; semiconductor rectifiers; dietary trace element (antioxidant)

name: Bromine
Key uses / notes: Flame retardants; photography (AgBr); pesticides; one of two liquid elements at RT

name: Krypton
Key uses / notes: Fluorescent lighting; lasers; KrF₂ exists; mostly inert noble gas

name: Rubidium
Key uses / notes: Atomic clocks (Rb standard); photoelectric cells; medical imaging tracers

name: Strontium
Key uses / notes: Fireworks (crimson red); ⁸⁹Sr/⁹⁰Sr geochronology; CRT phosphors

name: Yttrium
Key uses / notes: Yttrium aluminum garnet (YAG) lasers; YBCO superconductors; phosphors

name: Zirconium
Key uses / notes: Nuclear reactor cladding (low neutron cross-section); zirconia ceramics; gemstones

name: Niobium
Key uses / notes: Superalloy jet engines; superconductors; Aufbau anomaly (4d⁴ 5s¹)

name: Molybdenum
Key uses / notes: High-temp steel alloys; lubricants (MoS₂); Aufbau anomaly (4d⁵ 5s¹)

name: Technetium
Key uses / notes: First synthetic element; ⁹ₙmTc used in nuclear medicine imaging; all isotopes radioactive

name: Ruthenium
Key uses / notes: Hardening Pt/Pd alloys; catalysis; Aufbau anomaly (4d⁷ 5s¹)

name: Rhodium
Key uses / notes: Catalytic converters; thermocouple alloys; Aufbau anomaly (4d⁸ 5s¹)

name: Palladium
Key uses / notes: Catalytic converters; hydrogen storage; only transition metal with Aufbau anomaly (4d¹⁰)

name: Silver
Key uses / notes: Jewelry; photography (AgBr); electrical contacts; antibacterial; Aufbau anomaly (4d¹⁰ 5s¹)

name: Cadmium
Key uses / notes: Ni-Cd batteries; cadmium yellow pigment; PVC stabilizer; highly toxic

name: Indium
Key uses / notes: ITO transparent electrodes (touchscreens); low-melting solders; semiconductors

name: Tin
Key uses / notes: Tin cans (food preservation); bronze; solder; pewter; second most used metal in alloys

name: Antimony
Key uses / notes: Lead-acid battery electrodes; flame retardants; Sb₂O₃ catalyst

name: Tellurium
Key uses / notes: Thermoelectric devices (Bi₂Te₃); CdTe solar cells; semiconductor alloys

name: Iodine
Key uses / notes: Thyroid imaging and cancer treatment (¹³¹I); table salt iodization; antiseptic

name: Xenon
Key uses / notes: Anesthesia; XeF₂ etching agent; ion propulsion; excimer lasers

name: Cesium
Key uses / notes: Atomic clocks (Cs-133 defines the SI second); photoelectric effect; drilling fluids

name: Barium
Key uses / notes: Fireworks (green); barium sulfate X-ray contrast agent; rat poison

name: Lanthanum
Key uses / notes: La₂O₃ camera lenses; hydrogen storage alloys (LaNi₅); FCC cracking catalysts

name: Cerium
Key uses / notes: CeO₂ catalytic converters; self-cleaning ovens; Ce glass polishing

name: Praseodymium
Key uses / notes: Pr-Nd magnets (misch metal); Pr glass coloring (yellow-green); lasers

name: Neodymium
Key uses / notes: Nd-Fe-B strongest permanent magnets (motors, hard disks); Nd:YAG lasers

name: Promethium
Key uses / notes: Radioactive; no stable isotopes; promethium-147 beta source; nuclear batteries

name: Samarium
Key uses / notes: Sm-Co permanent magnets (high-temperature); neutron absorber in reactors; SmI₂ reductant

name: Europium
Key uses / notes: Eu red/blue phosphors in LED lighting; Eu²⁺ NMR shift reagents; Eu dosimetry

name: Gadolinium
Key uses / notes: MRI contrast agent (Gd-DTPA); Gd neutron absorber; Gd₂O₂S X-ray screens

name: Terbium
Key uses / notes: TbFe₂ magnetostrictive alloy (Terfenol-D); Tb green phosphors; solid oxide fuel cells

name: Dysprosium
Key uses / notes: Dysprosium in Nd-Fe-B magnets (reduces demagnetization); data storage; dosimetry

name: Holmium
Key uses / notes: Highest magnetic moment of any element; Ho:YAG lasers; nuclear medicine

name: Erbium
Key uses / notes: Er:YAG medical/dental lasers; Er-doped fiber amplifiers (EDFA); pink glass coloring

name: Thulium
Key uses / notes: Portable X-ray sources (Tm-170); Tm:YAG lasers; rarest lanthanide after Pm

name: Ytterbium
Key uses / notes: Yb:YAG/fiber lasers; Yb optical lattice clocks; stress gauges

name: Lutetium
Key uses / notes: Lu₂O₃ PET scintillators; Lu-177 cancer therapy; petroleum cracking catalyst

name: Hafnium
Key uses / notes: Nuclear reactor control rods; superalloy turbine blades; MOSFET gate dielectric (HfO₂)

name: Tantalum
Key uses / notes: Capacitors (Ta₂O₅); surgical implants; corrosion-resistant chemical equipment

name: Tungsten
Key uses / notes: Incandescent bulb filaments; TIG welding electrodes; highest melting point of all metals

name: Rhenium
Key uses / notes: Superalloy additive; thermocouples; last stable element discovered (1925)

name: Osmium
Key uses / notes: Hardening Pt alloys; OsO₄ biological stain; densest naturally occurring element

name: Iridium
Key uses / notes: Spark plug electrodes; crucibles; second densest element; Ir anomaly marks K-Pg boundary

name: Platinum
Key uses / notes: Catalytic converters (autocatalyst); jewelry; Pt electrodes; cancer drug (cisplatin); Aufbau anomaly

name: Gold
Key uses / notes: Jewelry; electronics; monetary standard; conductor; Aufbau anomaly (5d¹⁰ 6s¹)

name: Mercury
Key uses / notes: Fluorescent lamps; thermometers (historical); amalgam dental fillings; toxic heavy metal

name: Thallium
Key uses / notes: Tl₂SO₄ rodenticide (restricted); Tl-201 cardiac imaging; thallium glass

name: Lead
Key uses / notes: Lead-acid batteries; radiation shielding; historical plumbing; toxic; ²⁰⁶Pb geochronology

name: Bismuth
Key uses / notes: Bismuth subsalicylate (Pepto-Bismol); Bi₂O₃ in cosmetics; low-melting alloys; weakly radioactive

name: Polonium
Key uses / notes: Radioactive; Po-210 alpha source; antistatic devices; discovered by Marie Curie

name: Astatine
Key uses / notes: Radioactive; rarest naturally occurring element; At-211 targeted alpha therapy

name: Radon
Key uses / notes: Radioactive gas; geologic dating; second leading cause of lung cancer (soil emanation)

name: Francium
Key uses / notes: Radioactive; heaviest alkali metal; extremely rare; used in atomic/nuclear research

name: Radium
Key uses / notes: Radioactive; historical cancer treatment (radium needles); luminous paint (historical)

name: Actinium
Key uses / notes: Radioactive; Ac-225/Ac-227 for targeted alpha therapy; natural decay chain member

name: Thorium
Key uses / notes: Radioactive; Th-232 thorium fuel cycle; gas mantles; high-temp ceramics

name: Protactinium
Key uses / notes: Radioactive; decay product of U-235; Pa-231 in neutron cross-section studies

name: Uranium
Key uses / notes: Radioactive; nuclear fuel (U-235); depleted uranium armor; U-Pb geochronology

name: Neptunium
Key uses / notes: Radioactive; first transuranium element (1940); Np-237 produced in reactors

name: Plutonium
Key uses / notes: Radioactive; nuclear weapons (Pu-239); nuclear power reactors; Pu-238 RTG power

name: Americium
Key uses / notes: Radioactive; Am-241 in smoke detectors; Am-241/243 transmutation research

name: Curium
Key uses / notes: Radioactive; Cm-244 RTG source; named for Marie and Pierre Curie; Aufbau anomaly (5f⁷ 6d¹)

name: Berkelium
Key uses / notes: Radioactive; Bk-249 used to synthesize heavier elements; limited availability

name: Californium
Key uses / notes: Radioactive; Cf-252 neutron source for well logging and cancer therapy

name: Einsteinium
Key uses / notes: Radioactive; trace amounts in thermonuclear test debris; no practical uses

name: Fermium
Key uses / notes: Radioactive; heaviest element detectable in macroscopic quantities; no practical uses

name: Mendelevium
Key uses / notes: Radioactive; named for Mendeleev; synthesized atom by atom; no practical uses

name: Nobelium
Key uses / notes: Radioactive; No²⁺ unusually stable for actinide; no practical uses

name: Lawrencium
Key uses / notes: Radioactive; last actinide; 7p¹ in ground state (unusual); no practical uses

name: Rutherfordium
Key uses / notes: Synthetic; predicted homolog of Hf; no weighable amounts; T½(Rf-267) ≈1.3 h

name: Dubnium
Key uses / notes: Synthetic; predicted homolog of Ta; named for Dubna, Russia; T½(Db-268) ≈16 h

name: Seaborgium
Key uses / notes: Synthetic; first element named for a living person (Glenn Seaborg, 1997); T½(Sg-271) ≈2 min

name: Bohrium
Key uses / notes: Synthetic; predicted homolog of Re; T½(Bh-270) ≈61 s; few atoms ever produced

name: Hassium
Key uses / notes: Synthetic; named for Hesse, Germany; predicted to have volatile OsO₄-like tetroxide; T½ ~9 s

name: Meitnerium
Key uses / notes: Synthetic; named for Lise Meitner; T½(Mt-278) ≈4 s; chemistry largely unknown

name: Darmstadtium
Key uses / notes: Synthetic; named for Darmstadt, Germany; T½(Ds-281) ≈11 s; chemistry unknown

name: Roentgenium
Key uses / notes: Synthetic; named for Wilhelm Röntgen; T½(Rg-282) ≈80 s; predicted relativistic Au-like chemistry

name: Copernicium
Key uses / notes: Synthetic; named for Copernicus; predicted to be a gas at RT due to relativistic effects; T½ ~29 s

name: Nihonium
Key uses / notes: Synthetic; first element discovered in Asia (RIKEN, Japan, 2004); T½(Nh-286) ~20 s

name: Flerovium
Key uses / notes: Synthetic; predicted semi-noble behavior (relativistic 7p²); named for Flerov Laboratory; T½ ~0.97 s

name: Moscovium
Key uses / notes: Synthetic; named for Moscow Oblast; T½(Mc-290) ≈65 ms; predicted homolog of Bi

name: Livermorium
Key uses / notes: Synthetic; named for Livermore, California; T½(Lv-293) ≈61 ms; predicted homolog of Po

name: Tennessine
Key uses / notes: Synthetic; named for Tennessee; heaviest known halogen; T½(Ts-294) ≈51 ms

name: Oganesson
Key uses / notes: Synthetic; heaviest known element; named for Yuri Oganessian; T½(Og-294) ≈0.69 ms; possibly solid
